summary: DeVault argues that Collabora added HDCP (Digital Restrictions Management) support to the Weston Wayland compositor purely for financial reasons, despite their blog post claiming it benefits users. He contends that DRM is universally understood to be technically ineffective, yet causes real harm by restricting user freedoms and enabling media companies to abuse consumers. He draws a parallel to Mozilla adding DRM to Firefox in 2014, which failed to halt Chrome's rise. DeVault calls on engineers to refuse unethical directives and urges Collabora to revert the changes.
key_points: DRM is technically broken and universally acknowledged as ineffective — HDCP can be defeated with a $100 Amazon device
Even though HDCP support doesn't directly restrict users, it enables media companies to impose restrictions on them through free software
Mozilla's capitulation on DRM in Firefox did nothing to stop Chrome's dominance, proving that appeasing DRM advocates yields no strategic benefit
Software engineers have real power to refuse unethical work and should exercise it — DeVault claims to have talked other developers down from implementing DRM
Free software maintainers have a responsibility to protect users from corporate abuse, and implementing DRM in free software is a betrayal of that duty
money_quotes: Q: Why was HDCP added to Weston? A: $$$$$
No reasonable engineer would knowingly agree to adding a broken system like that to their system, and trust me - the entire engineering community has been made well-aware of these faults.
Adding HDCP support is handing a bottle of whiskey to an abusive husband.
This feature enables media companies to abuse your users.
You are not the lowly code monkey you may think you are. Engineers have real power in the organization.
You can say 'no' and it's your responsibility to say 'no' when someone asks you to write unethical code.
Anyone who doesn't recognize that self-interested organizations with a great deal of resources are working against our interests as a free software community is an idiot.

Anyone who doesn't stand up to their boss, sits down, implements it in our free software ecosystem, and cashes their check the next Friday - is not only a coward, but a traitor to their users, their peers, and to society as a whole.
key_insight: Implementing DRM in free software — even when it doesn't directly restrict users — is a betrayal of the free software community because it enables the very corporate actors working to undermine user freedom.
